task: I’m trying to get a small vendor setup approved without bouncing between departments forever. Can you help me map the usual steps and what paperwork I should have ready so I don’t get stuck waiting on avoidable back-and-forth?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — here’s the usual path and the paperwork that tends to prevent the most back-and-forth.

Typical vendor setup flow:
1. Request intake
2. Vendor due diligence / onboarding review
3. Tax and payment setup
4. Insurance / compliance review
5. Contract / scope review
6. Approval routing
7. Vendor creation in ERP / AP system
8. First invoice / payment readiness

Paperwork to have ready:
- Vendor legal name exactly as registered
- DBA name, if different
- Address, phone, email, website
- Tax form:
  - W-9 for US vendors
  - W-8 series for non-US vendors
- EIN / tax ID
- Banking details for ACH/wire:
  - bank name
  - routing number
  - account number
  - account type
  - remittance email
- Certificate of insurance, if required
- Signed contract / MSA / SOW / PO, if applicable
- Scope of work or service description
- Pricing / rate sheet
- Primary contact and AP contact
- Vendor questionnaire / onboarding form, if your company uses one
- Diversity / compliance forms, if required
- Sanctions / conflict / ethics certifications, if required

What usually causes delays:
- Mismatch between legal name, tax form, and bank account name
- Missing W-9/W-8
- Insurance limits not meeting policy
- No approved contract or scope
- Unclear approver for the spend
- Missing cost center / project code
- Bank info submitted in an insecure or incomplete way
- Vendor not marked as approved by procurement/compliance before AP tries to create it

Best way to avoid bouncing around:
- Ask one person or one inbox to confirm the full required packet
- Submit everything in one package
- Include a short cover note with:
  - what the vendor does
  - expected spend
  - start date
  - who approved the need
  - where invoices should be coded
- Ask for a single point of contact for exceptions

If you want, I can turn this into:
- a one-page vendor onboarding checklist, or
- a short email template you can send internally to get the exact required packet in one shot.

user_simulator.text:
Mostly right. But I need this tighter and more usable — the part that actually keeps things from stalling is the order, not just the list.

I’d want it broken into:
- who has to touch it first
- what has to be complete before it leaves that desk
- what gets AP kicked back

Also, in real life the paper trail usually dies on the approval side, not the vendor side. Missing cost center, wrong approver, no scope attached, insurance not matching the contract — that’s where it drags.

Give me a cleaner step-by-step with:
1. intake
2. procurement/compliance
3. AP/tax/banking
4. contract/insurance
5. final approval

And put a blunt “kickback risk” note under each. That’s the part I’d actually use.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — here’s the tighter version in the order you want, with the real kickback points.

## 1) Intake
**Who touches it first:** requester / business owner / admin  
**Must be complete before it leaves this desk:**
- Vendor legal name
- What they’re being hired to do
- Expected spend
- Start date
- Cost center / project code
- Who is requesting and who is approving
- Any deadline or business reason

**Kickback risk:**
- Missing cost center
- No clear business owner
- Scope too vague
- Requester assumes AP/procurement will “figure it out”

---

## 2) Procurement / Compliance
**Who touches it:** procurement, compliance, legal, risk, or whoever screens vendors  
**Must be complete before it leaves this desk:**
- Scope of work attached
- Correct approver identified
- Any required vendor questionnaire
- Conflict / sanctions / ethics checks, if used
- Insurance requirement confirmed if applicable
- Contract path confirmed: no contract, MSA, SOW, or PO

**Kickback risk:**
- No scope attached
- Wrong approval chain
- Vendor is being treated as “simple” when compliance review is required
- Insurance or risk requirements don’t match the work
- Scope and approval don’t line up

---

## 3) AP / Tax / Banking
**Who touches it:** AP, vendor master, finance ops  
**Must be complete before it leaves this desk:**
- W-9 or W-8
- Legal name matches tax form
- Banking details complete and consistent
- Remittance contact/email
- Payment method confirmed
- Any vendor setup form completed

**Kickback risk:**
- Name mismatch between legal name, tax form, and bank account
- Missing W-9/W-8
- Incomplete bank info
- Banking sent in an unsecured or unusable format
- AP can’t create the vendor because compliance hasn’t cleared it yet

---

## 4) Contract / Insurance
**Who touches it:** legal, procurement, risk, insurance reviewer  
**Must be complete before it leaves this desk:**
- Signed contract / MSA / SOW / PO, as required
- Scope matches the request
- Insurance certificate matches contract terms
- Coverage limits and named parties are correct
- Any redlines resolved or approved

**Kickback risk:**
- Insurance doesn’t match the contract
- Contract says one thing, scope says another
- Unsigned or partially signed paperwork
- Work starts before contract is fully approved
- Vendor is set up before legal/risk clears the terms

---

## 5) Final Approval
**Who touches it:** budget owner / department head / delegated approver  
**Must be complete before it leaves this desk:**
- All prior steps cleared
- Budget confirmed
- Cost center/project code confirmed
- Final approver matches delegation rules
- No open exceptions

**Kickback risk:**
- Wrong approver
- Approval comes from someone without authority
- Budget code missing or wrong
- Final approval is given before compliance/contract/AP are done
- One missing attachment sends it back to the start

## Best way to avoid stalls
- Get the **approval chain** right first
- Attach the **scope/contract** before routing
- Make sure **cost center + approver + vendor name** all match
- Don’t send to AP until **tax/banking and compliance** are clean
- Use one packet, not piecemeal submissions

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page internal routing checklist** with checkboxes and a “send back if missing” column.

user_simulator.text:
Better. Still needs one more layer: a real routing view, not just desk-by-desk. The part that matters is who can stop it and what has to be true before it moves.

I’d want it like this:
- **Step owner**
- **Required before handoff**
- **Common kickback**
- **Who can actually approve / reject**

Because in practice, the choke point is usually one of three things:
1. wrong approver,
2. missing scope or contract attachment,
3. AP can’t touch it because procurement/compliance didn’t clear it.

Also, your Step 3 and Step 4 overlap a little. Banking/tax is AP, but contract/insurance often blocks AP from even opening the vendor. So I’d separate **setup eligibility** from **payment setup** more sharply.

If you’re willing, tighten it one more notch into a table with columns:
- stage
- owner
- must-have docs
- approval gate
- kickback risk
- what to fix first

That’s the version I’d actually use to keep people from bouncing it around.

turns.agent_response:
| Stage | Owner | Must-have docs | Approval gate | Kickback risk | What to fix first |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Intake | Requester / business owner | Vendor legal name, service description, expected spend, start date, cost center / project code, requester + business approver | Business need is clear and coded correctly | Wrong cost center, vague scope, missing owner, wrong approver listed | Fix the approver and cost center first |
| 2. Setup eligibility | Procurement / compliance / legal / risk | Scope of work, vendor questionnaire, conflict/sanctions/ethics forms if required, contract path, insurance requirement | Vendor is allowed to proceed to setup review | No scope attached, wrong approval chain, compliance not cleared, insurance not aligned to work | Attach the scope/contract and clear compliance blockers |
| 3. Payment setup | AP / vendor master / finance ops | W-9 or W-8, legal name match, banking details, remittance contact, vendor setup form | AP can create the vendor record | Missing tax form, name mismatch, incomplete bank info, AP blocked by upstream review | Fix tax form and bank details, then confirm upstream clearance |
| 4. Contract / insurance | Legal / procurement / risk | Signed MSA / SOW / PO as required, COI, endorsements if needed, redlines resolved | Contract and insurance match the work | Insurance doesn’t match contract, unsigned docs, scope and contract conflict | Fix contract language and insurance alignment |
| 5. Final approval | Budget owner / department head / delegated approver | All prior steps cleared, budget confirmed, final approver authority verified | Final business approval to activate | Wrong approver, missing authority, open exceptions, budget code wrong | Verify approver authority and budget code before routing |
| 6. Vendor creation / release | AP / vendor master | All approved packet docs, final clearances, payment setup complete | Vendor can be activated in ERP / AP system | Packet incomplete, upstream hold still open, setup not fully cleared | Close every hold before release |

**Fast rule:**  
If it’s bouncing, check these first:
1. **Wrong approver**
2. **Missing scope / contract attachment**
3. **AP blocked because procurement / compliance hasn’t cleared it**
4. **Cost center missing or wrong**
5. **Insurance doesn’t match the contract**

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page routing checklist** with a “stop here / send back / move forward” format.

user_simulator.text:
Closer. But that still reads like a generic process map, not the routing control sheet I asked for.

I want the choke points made explicit in the table itself — not buried in a fast rule at the bottom.

Specifically:
- add a column for **who can stop it**
- make **setup eligibility** and **payment setup** separate enough that AP isn’t shown as owning anything it can’t actually touch
- put the **approval gate** in plain language, not corporate fluff
- and for each stage, say what gets it kicked back **first** — because that’s what people fix under pressure

Also, stage 6 feels unnecessary unless you’re treating vendor master activation as its own gate. If so, keep it. If not, fold it into AP and stop pretending there’s a separate clean handoff.

I’d want the next pass to be even more operational:
- stage
- owner
- who can stop it
- must-have docs
- approval gate
- kickback risk
- fix first

No extra explanation. Just the table, clean.

turns.agent_response:
| Stage | Owner | Who can stop it | Must-have docs | Approval gate | Kickback risk | Fix first |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Intake | Requester / business owner | Requester, budget owner, admin coordinator | Vendor legal name, service description, expected spend, start date, cost center / project code, requester, business approver | Request is complete enough to route | Wrong approver, missing cost center, vague scope, no business owner | Fix approver and cost center first |
| 2. Setup eligibility | Procurement / compliance / legal / risk | Procurement, compliance, legal, risk, sometimes budget owner | Scope of work, vendor questionnaire, conflict / sanctions / ethics forms if required, contract path, insurance requirement if applicable | Vendor is allowed to move into setup review | No scope attached, wrong approval chain, compliance not cleared, insurance not aligned to the work | Attach scope/contract and clear compliance blockers first |
| 3. Payment setup | AP / vendor master / finance ops | AP, vendor master, finance ops | W-9 or W-8, legal name match, banking details, remittance contact, vendor setup form | AP can create the payment record | Missing tax form, name mismatch, incomplete bank info, AP blocked by upstream clearance | Fix tax form and bank details first, then confirm upstream clearance |
| 4. Contract / insurance | Legal / procurement / risk | Legal, procurement, risk, insurance reviewer | Signed MSA / SOW / PO as required, COI, endorsements if needed, redlines resolved | Contract and insurance match the work | Insurance doesn’t match contract, unsigned docs, scope and contract conflict | Fix contract language and insurance alignment first |
| 5. Final approval | Budget owner / department head / delegated approver | Final approver only, plus anyone with delegated authority rules | All prior steps cleared, budget confirmed, final approver authority verified | Final business approval to proceed | Wrong approver, missing authority, open exceptions, wrong budget code | Verify approver authority and budget code first |
| 6. Vendor master activation / release | AP / vendor master | AP, vendor master, system control owner | Fully approved packet, all prior clearances, payment setup complete | Vendor can be activated in ERP / AP system | Packet incomplete, hold still open, setup not fully cleared | Close every hold before release |

user_simulator.text:
Closer. 6 is still a little fuzzy.

If you keep it, make the gate and stop-point more explicit for vendor master activation — that’s the part people actually trip on. Otherwise fold it into step 3 and don’t split hairs.

Also, I’d want one more thing in the table: whether the stage is a **hard stop** or just a **review stop**. Because some of these aren’t equal. AP can’t move without clearance, but budget owners can still reject on authority alone.

So: add a simple **stop type** column, or make the who-can-stop-it column clear enough that it shows who can hard-block versus who only reviews. That’s the last bit before this is actually usable.

turns.agent_response:
| Stage | Owner | Who can stop it | Stop type | Must-have docs | Approval gate | Kickback risk | Fix first |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Intake | Requester / business owner | Requester, budget owner, admin coordinator | Review stop | Vendor legal name, service description, expected spend, start date, cost center / project code, requester, business approver | Request is complete enough to route | Wrong approver, missing cost center, vague scope, no business owner | Fix approver and cost center first |
| 2. Setup eligibility | Procurement / compliance / legal / risk | Procurement, compliance, legal, risk, sometimes budget owner | Hard stop | Scope of work, vendor questionnaire, conflict / sanctions / ethics forms if required, contract path, insurance requirement if applicable | Vendor is allowed to move into setup review | No scope attached, wrong approval chain, compliance not cleared, insurance not aligned to the work | Attach scope/contract and clear compliance blockers first |
| 3. Payment setup | AP / vendor master / finance ops | AP, vendor master, finance ops | Hard stop | W-9 or W-8, legal name match, banking details, remittance contact, vendor setup form | AP can create the payment record | Missing tax form, name mismatch, incomplete bank info, AP blocked by upstream clearance | Fix tax form and bank details first, then confirm upstream clearance |
| 4. Contract / insurance | Legal / procurement / risk | Legal, procurement, risk, insurance reviewer | Hard stop | Signed MSA / SOW / PO as required, COI, endorsements if needed, redlines resolved | Contract and insurance match the work | Insurance doesn’t match contract, unsigned docs, scope and contract conflict | Fix contract language and insurance alignment first |
| 5. Final approval | Budget owner / department head / delegated approver | Final approver only, plus anyone with delegated authority rules | Hard stop | All prior steps cleared, budget confirmed, final approver authority verified | Final business approval to proceed | Wrong approver, missing authority, open exceptions, wrong budget code | Verify approver authority and budget code first |
| 6. Vendor master activation / release | AP / vendor master | AP, vendor master, system control owner | Hard stop | Fully approved packet, all prior clearances, payment setup complete | Vendor can be activated in ERP / AP system | Packet incomplete, hold still open, setup not fully cleared | Close every hold before release |

user_simulator.text: Yeah. That’s the usable version. The stop type makes it actually operational instead of just descriptive. I’d use this.
